summary: Wes frames open source tool building as a response to the pain of working with data, arguing that firsthand scientific and analytical experience is essential for designing good ergonomics. He highlights a shift toward a richer, more modular ecosystem where specialized libraries can own concerns like table styling, avoiding tightly coupled releases inside core projects like pandas. He traces how experiments with lazy expression APIs across projects like Ibis and Siuba influenced today’s DataFrame tooling, including Polars and compatibility layers. He sees AI as a watershed comparable to the advent of the internet and expects LLM discoverability to strongly shape adoption of new open source projects. He also stresses that enterprise constraints and governance needs create a persistent demand for tooling that bridges open source innovation and organizational realities.
key_points: Tool builders need domain experience to design ergonomics that genuinely improve data workflows.
The ecosystem is moving toward modularity, enabling projects to delegate features instead of embedding them in core libraries.
Lazy expression experiments across projects cross-pollinated and shaped modern DataFrame APIs.
AI and LLM indexing will materially affect how new open source tools gain adoption.
Enterprise governance and security constraints remain a major driver for tooling beyond pure open source.
money_quotes: But I was also driven, attracted to the tool building aspect because of how difficult it was to work with data.
And I found that just building tools and accelerating workflows and making people more productive was really satisfying.
And so it's an easier decision to make to say, let's delegate out to a library like Great Tables rather than Polars implementing all of its table styling itself.
My experience, six months is my expectation for when I can expect to really start getting some feedback about building something new.
And I think that all of this work definitely, directly or indirectly influenced the design of Polars and Polars' expression system, which I think works really well and people seem to be happy with.
It feels like as big of a change as going from no internet to internet in many ways.

There's going to be a growing segment of users that if you're building a new open source project, people aren't going to use your open source project until they can use it via vibe coding essentially, or until it gets indexed by the LLMs.
key_insight: Wes argues that durable data tools come from hard-earned workflow pain and are now shaped by a modular open source ecosystem and an AI-driven shift in how users discover and adopt software.
